title: India-US Interim Trade Deal in Final Stages; USTR Greer to Visit New Delhi on June 23-24
summary: The India-US interim trade deal requires only 'final touches' as per official statements, with US Trade Representative Jamieson Greer scheduled to visit New Delhi on June 23-24 for ministerial-level talks. The two sides have been negotiating a Bilateral Trade Agreement (BTA) amid US tariff pressures. Discussions at the G7 summit also featured Modi-Trump interactions covering trade, tariffs, and strategic trust-building. The deal aims to address tariff differentials and expand market access for both sides.
exam_angle: India-US trade relations are a recurring GS Paper 2 (IR) topic; understanding BTA negotiations, the role of USTR, and the context of US reciprocal tariffs imposed in 2025 is essential for Mains and interviews.
key_facts: USTR Jamieson Greer to visit New Delhi: June 23-24
Deal described as needing only 'final touches'
Modi-Trump bilateral at G7 covered trade, tariffs, and trust
Negotiations are for a Bilateral Trade Agreement (BTA)
source: The Hindu / Times of India / India Today

title: India-EU Free Trade Agreement to be Signed by December 2025; Implementation from February-March 2026
summary: Commerce Minister Piyush Goyal announced that the India-EU Free Trade Agreement (FTA) is expected to be signed by December 2025, with implementation beginning from February-March 2026. The FTA negotiations, formally known as the India-EU Trade and Investment Agreement (BTIA), had been stalled for over a decade before resuming in 2022. The agreement covers goods, services, investment, and intellectual property.
exam_angle: India-EU BTIA is a significant bilateral trade topic for GS Paper 2; the timeline, key sectors involved, and the background of stalled negotiations since 2013 are important for Mains.
key_facts: FTA signing expected: December 2025
Implementation targeted: February-March 2026
Announced by: Commerce Minister Piyush Goyal
Negotiations resumed in 2022 after decade-long stall

title: US-Iran Nuclear Talks Begin in Switzerland; Lebanon Conflict Also on Agenda
summary: The United States and Iran began direct negotiations in Switzerland aimed at reaching a new nuclear deal, with both sides expressing willingness to 'turn over a new leaf'. Iran confirmed that the Lebanon conflict situation was also a top agenda item during the talks. The negotiations come amid ongoing US threats of harder sanctions and Iran's warnings of retaliation. Switzerland is acting as a neutral venue for the high-stakes diplomatic engagement.
exam_angle: US-Iran nuclear negotiations are directly relevant to GS Paper 2 (IR) — connects to the JCPOA framework, nuclear non-proliferation (NPT), and Middle East geopolitics frequently asked in Mains and Prelims.
key_facts: Venue: Switzerland (neutral ground)
Agenda: Nuclear deal + Lebanon conflict
Context: US threatened harder sanctions; Iran warned of consequences
Relates to: JCPOA (Joint Comprehensive Plan of Action) framework

title: Supreme Court Rules on Right to Walk on Footpaths; Congress Seeks Fundamental Right to Vote
summary: The Supreme Court of India issued an order recognising citizens' right to walk on footpaths, citing a surge in pedestrian deaths in India and the duty of the state to ensure safe public infrastructure. The ruling draws on Article 21 (Right to Life) of the Constitution, extending it to include the right to safe public movement. In a related political development, the Congress party cited this ruling to demand recognition of the right to vote as a fundamental right. The order highlights the judiciary's expanding interpretation of Article 21.
exam_angle: Expansion of Article 21 by the Supreme Court is a core GS Paper 2 (Polity) topic; the right to life has been progressively interpreted to include quality of life, safe environment, and public infrastructure access — important for Prelims and Mains.
key_facts: Article invoked: Article 21 (Right to Life and Personal Liberty)
Issue: Right to walk safely on public footpaths
Context: Rising pedestrian deaths in India
Congress demanded: Fundamental Right to Vote via similar reasoning

title: Police Do Not Need Court Permission for Further Investigation After Filing Final Report: Legal Clarity
summary: The Hindu carried an analysis clarifying the legal position that police do not require court permission to conduct further investigation after filing a final report (chargesheet or closure report) under the Code of Criminal Procedure (CrPC) / Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita (BNSS). This is governed by Section 173(8) of the CrPC (now BNSS equivalent), which allows further investigation as a matter of right. The issue has significance for criminal procedure jurisprudence and has been the subject of multiple Supreme Court interpretations.
exam_angle: Criminal procedure provisions under CrPC/BNSS are relevant for GS Paper 2 (Polity & Governance); Section 173 and the concept of further investigation vs. re-investigation are frequently tested in law-related questions.
key_facts: Relevant provision: Section 173(8) CrPC / BNSS equivalent
Police can conduct further investigation without court permission after final report
Distinction: Further investigation ≠ Re-investigation (latter needs court order)
Governed by: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita (BNSS) 2023 replacing CrPC

title: India to Host BRICS Energy Ministers' Meeting in Gurugram on June 25-26
summary: India will host the BRICS Energy Ministers' Meeting in Gurugram on June 25-26, 2025, as part of India's BRICS chairmanship activities. The meeting is expected to focus on energy security, clean energy transitions, and cooperation among BRICS nations on sustainable development. India holds the BRICS Presidency in 2025 and has been organising multiple ministerial-level meetings across sectors.
exam_angle: BRICS-related events under India's Presidency are important for GS Paper 2 (IR); energy security and multilateral energy diplomacy are also relevant to GS Paper 3 (Economy/Environment).
key_facts: Event: BRICS Energy Ministers' Meeting
Venue: Gurugram, India
Date: June 25-26, 2025
India holds BRICS Presidency in 2025

title: AIIA Celebrates 12th International Day of Yoga 2025
summary: The All India Institute of Ayurveda (AIIA), New Delhi, celebrated the 12th International Day of Yoga with diverse wellness activities and enthusiastic participation across its campus. International Day of Yoga is observed annually on June 21, following India's initiative at the United Nations General Assembly in 2014 (Resolution 69/131), with the first celebration held in 2015. The theme for IDY 2025 continues the Ministry of Ayush's focus on yoga for holistic well-being.
exam_angle: International Day of Yoga — its origin via UNGA Resolution, India's role, and the nodal ministry (Ministry of Ayush) — is a standard Prelims fact; AIIA as an apex Ayurveda institution is also relevant.
key_facts: International Day of Yoga: June 21 annually
12th IDY celebrated in 2025 (first was 2015)
UNGA Resolution: 69/131 (2014), proposed by India
Nodal Ministry: Ministry of Ayush; AIIA = All India Institute of Ayurveda

title: India's Sovereign AI Strategy Debated: Government Weighs Sovereign AI Models vs. Integrated Approach
summary: Business Standard reported an analysis of India's national AI strategy, questioning whether developing sovereign (government-owned) AI models is the most effective approach for India. The debate centres on India's IndiaAI Mission (approved with Rs 10,372 crore outlay) and whether sovereign large language models (LLMs) or a hybrid public-private approach better serves national interests. Critics argue that sovereign models may lack competitiveness while proponents cite data sovereignty and strategic autonomy concerns.
exam_angle: India's AI policy, the IndiaAI Mission, and debates around digital sovereignty are highly relevant for GS Paper 3 (Sci-Tech) and Essays; connects to broader issues of technology governance and data localisation.
key_facts: IndiaAI Mission budget: Rs 10,372 crore (approved 2024)
Key debate: Sovereign AI models vs. public-private partnership approach
Concerns: Data sovereignty, strategic autonomy, global competitiveness
Nodal body: MeitY / IndiaAI (under Ministry of Electronics & IT)

title: Trump Administration Removes Anthropic from National Security Threat List
summary: US President Donald Trump stated he no longer views Anthropic — the AI safety company behind the Claude AI model — as a national security threat. This reversal follows earlier US government scrutiny of AI companies with foreign investment links. Anthropic's AI model 'Claude' (referred to in some reports as 'Fable 5') had also briefly disappeared from certain platforms, raising regulatory questions. The development reflects the evolving US approach to regulating frontier AI companies.
exam_angle: Regulation of frontier AI companies and the US approach to AI governance is relevant for GS Paper 3 (Sci-Tech) and GS Paper 2 (IR — technology diplomacy); connects to India's own AI regulatory framework discussions.
key_facts: Anthropic: US-based AI safety company, maker of Claude AI model
Trump declared Anthropic no longer a national security threat
Earlier scrutiny: Foreign investment links raised US security concerns
Relevant context: US CHIPS & Science Act, AI Executive Orders on national security

title: AI Agents Reshaping India's IT Services Workforce: Industry Analysis
summary: Business Standard published an analysis highlighting how AI agents — autonomous software systems capable of performing complex multi-step tasks — are creating a 'silent workforce shift' across India's IT services industry. Major Indian IT firms (TCS, Infosys, Wipro, HCLTech) are deploying AI agents to automate coding, testing, and client management tasks, raising concerns about employment displacement. The shift represents a structural change in the $250 billion Indian IT exports industry.
exam_angle: The impact of AI on employment and India's IT sector is relevant for GS Paper 3 (Economy/Sci-Tech) and Essay; connects to debates on technological unemployment, reskilling, and India's service sector competitiveness.
key_facts: AI agents: Autonomous systems performing multi-step tasks without human intervention
India's IT exports industry: ~$250 billion
Key concern: Structural employment displacement in IT services
Major firms affected: TCS, Infosys, Wipro, HCLTech
